message.content: read BUS0, especially the section we just added concerning the BURN aspects.  Also, read AXLA and MCM: I am musing we ought to better annotate tabtargets.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

206	
207	== Overview
208	
209	The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.
210	
211	BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.
212	
213	For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.
214	
215	== Tabtarget Vesture
216	
217	[[bust_tabtarget]]
218	//axl_voices axo_entity
219	{bust_tabtarget}::
220	Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
221	Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
222	+
223	* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
224	* separator: `.`
225	* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
226	* envelope: `.sh`
227	+
228	Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
229	+
230	Examples:
231	+
232	* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
233	* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
234	* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
235	* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation
236	
237	== Dispatch
238	
239	The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
240	A
241	{busdl_launcher}
242	validates arguments and delegates to a
243	{busdm_formulary}.
244	The
245	{busdc_colophon}
246	identifies the routing path;
247	the
248	{busdf_frontispiece}
249	provides human-readable description;
250	the
251	{busdi_imprint}
252	embeds target parameters.
253	
254	[[busdc_colophon]]
255	//axl_voices axt_string
256	{busdc_colophon}::
257	The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
258	A
259	{busdc_colophon}
260	determines which
261	{busdm_formulary}
262	handles the dispatch.
263	Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
264	+
265	The
266	{busdc_colophon}
267	is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
268	+
269	Examples:
270	+
271	* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
272	* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
273	* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench
274	
275	[[busdf_frontispiece]]
276	//axl_voices axt_string
277	{busdf_frontispiece}::
278	The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
279	A
280	{busdf_frontispiece}
281	uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
282	+
283	Appears after the
284	{busdc_colophon}
285	and dot separator.
286	+
287	Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.
288	
289	[[busdi_imprint]]
290	//axl_voices axt_string
291	{busdi_imprint}::
292	An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
293	An
294	{busdi_imprint}
295	specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
296	+
297	Appears after a second dot separator following the
298	{busdf_frontispiece}.
299	+
300	Examples:
301	+
302	* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
303	* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`
304	
305	[[busdm_formulary]]
306	//axl_voices axo_entity
307	{busdm_formulary}::
308	A component that routes
309	{busdc_colophon_s}
310	to implementations.
311	Each
312	{busdm_formulary}
313	registers handlers for a set of
314	{busdc_colophon_s}
315	and dispatches to the appropriate function.
316	+
317	Types:
318	+
319	* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
320	* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
321	+
322	A
323	{busdm_formulary}
324	is sourced by the
325	{busdl_launcher}
326	and provides the dispatch function.
327	
328	[[busdl_launcher]]
329	//axl_voices axo_entity
330	{busdl_launcher}::
331	A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
332	{busdm_formulary}.
333	A
334	{busdl_launcher}
335	is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
336	+
337	Naming follows
338	{bust_tabtarget}:
339	`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
340	+
341	Responsibilities:
342	+
343	* Parse
344	{busdc_colophon}
345	from own filename
346	* Validate required arguments
347	* Source the target
348	{busdm_formulary}
349	* Invoke the dispatched function
350	
351	== Routing Behavior
352	
353	=== Dispatch Flow
354	
355	. User invokes launcher: `./tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
356	. Launcher parses its filename to extract:
357	* {busdc_colophon}:
358	`rbw-cr`
359	* {busdf_frontispiece}:
360	`Rack`
361	* {busdi_imprint}:
362	`tadmor`
363	. Launcher sources the appropriate
364	{busdm_formulary}
365	(`rbw_workbench.sh`)
366	. Formulary's dispatch function routes based on frontispiece
367	. Target function executes with imprint as parameter
368	
369	=== Workbench Pattern
370	
371	[source,bash]
372	----
373	# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}w_workbench.sh
374	
375	{prefix}w_dispatch() {
376	    local frontispiece="$1"
377	    local imprint="$2"
378	    shift 2
379	
380	    case "$frontispiece" in
381	        Build)          {prefix}w_build "$imprint" "$@" ;;
382	        Rack)           {prefix}w_rack "$imprint" "$@" ;;
383	        *)              buc_fatal "Unknown frontispiece: $frontispiece" ;;
384	    esac
385	}
386	----
387	
388	=== Testbench Pattern
389	
390	Testbenches follow the same pattern but route to test functions:
391	
392	[source,bash]
393	----
394	# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}t_testbench.sh
395	
396	{prefix}t_dispatch() {
397	    local frontispiece="$1"
398	    shift
399	
400	    case "$frontispiece" in
401	        Unit)        {prefix}t_unit "$@" ;;
402	        Integration) {prefix}t_integration "$@" ;;
403	        *)           buc_fatal "Unknown test: $frontispiece" ;;
404	    esac
405	}
406	----
407	
408	=== Test Registry Model
409	
410	The test registry (`butr_registry.sh`) uses two orthogonal enrollment dimensions:
411	
412	**Fixture** — init/setup execution context.
413	Each test case belongs to exactly one fixture.
414	Fixtures provide isolation via `_tsuite` subshell boundaries.
415	Registered with `butr_fixture_enroll(name, init_fn, setup_fn)`.
416	
417	**Suite** — cross-cutting selection group (N:M with cases).
418	A case can belong to multiple suites.
419	Suites classify by dependency tier (what environment must exist), not by test methodology.
420	A suite is a membership tag.
421	Suites enable suite dispatch: run a cross-cutting subset of cases grouped by their owning fixtures.
422	Suite context is set with `butr_suite_enroll(SUITE1 [SUITE2...])` before case enrollments.
423	Queried by `butd_run_suite(suite_name)`.
424	
425	Standard suite constants (defined as `readonly` in testbench kindle):
426	
427	* `BUTR_SUITE_FAST` — no external dependencies, CI-safe
428	* `BUTR_SUITE_SERVICE` — needs credentials, no container runtime
429	* `BUTR_SUITE_COMPLETE` — full test matrix including container runtime
430	
431	Dispatch entry points:
432	
433	* `butd_run_all()` — all fixtures, all cases
434	* `butd_run_fixture(name [case...])` — single fixture, optional case filter
435	* `butd_run_one(fn)` — single case with its fixture setup
436	* `butd_run_suite(suite)` — cross-cutting suite, grouped by fixture
437	
438	=== Colophon Conventions
439	
440	{busdc_colophon_s}
441	follow a structure that encodes both kit identity and formulary type:
442	
443	* `{cipher}-` — default workbench (e.g., `rbw-`, `jjw-`)
444	* `{cipher}-t-` — testbench (e.g., `buw-t-`, `jjw-t-`)
445	* `{cipher}-{variant}-` — specialized workbench (e.g., `rbw-cr-` for crucible rack operations)
446	
447	The trailing hyphen is always part of the colophon — it separates the routing prefix from the dot-delimited tabtarget components.
448	
449	== Test Invocation
450	
451	The test invocation vocabulary defines how test cases exercise code under controlled capture.
452	All invocation tiers share a common primitive,
453	`zbuto_invoke`,
454	which captures stdout, stderr, exit status, and optional BURV output into
455	{busig_stdout},
456	{busig_stderr},
457	{busig_status},
458	and
459	{busig_burv_output}.
460	
461	=== Invocation Tiers
462	
463	Three tiers provide increasing levels of resolution:
464	
465	[[busit_unit]]
466	//axl_voices axo_entity
467	{busit_unit}::
468	Invokes a raw function or command directly via `zbuto_invoke`.
469	No tabtarget file or workbench routing involved.
470	+
471	API: `buto_unit_expect_ok(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_fatal(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_ok_stdout(expected, command...)`
472	
473	[[busit_tabtarget]]
474	//axl_voices axo_entity
475	{busit_tabtarget}::
476	Resolves a
477	{busdc_colophon}
478	to a
479	{busdl_launcher}
480	file in `tt/`, then invokes it via `zbuto_invoke`.
481	Dies if no tabtarget matches or if multiple matches exist.
482	+
483	API: `buto_tt_expect_ok(colophon, args...)`, `buto_tt_expect_fatal(colophon, args...)`
484	
485	[[busit_launch]]
486	//axl_voices axo_entity
487	{busit_launch}::
488	Invokes a workbench dispatch function directly, bypassing the
489	{busdl_launcher}
490	file.
491	Useful when exercising workbench routing without a tabtarget on disk.
492	+
493	API: `buto_launch_expect_ok(launcher, colophon, args...)`, `buto_launch_expect_fatal(launcher, colophon, args...)`
494	
495	=== Capture Globals
496	
497	Every call to `zbuto_invoke` sets four globals that subsequent assertions can inspect.
498	
499	[[busig_stdout]]
500	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
501	{busig_stdout}::
502	Captured stdout from the invoked command.
503	
504	[[busig_stderr]]
505	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
506	{busig_stderr}::
507	Captured stderr from the invoked command.
508	
509	[[busig_status]]
510	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
511	{busig_status}::
512	Exit code from the invoked command.
513	
514	[[busig_burv_output]]
515	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
516	{busig_burv_output}::
517	BURV output directory for the invocation.
518	Empty when
519	{busx_burv_root}
520	is not set.
521	When set, contains the invocation's isolated output tree rooted at
522	`{BUTE_BURV_ROOT}/invoke-NNNNN/output`.

524	=== Case Boundary
525	
526	[[busx_tcase]]
527	//axl_voices axo_entity
528	{busx_tcase}::
529	The case isolation runner (`zbute_tcase`).
530	Executes a single test case function in a subshell with per-case temporary state.
531	Sets
532	{busx_temp_dir}
533	and
534	{busx_burv_root}
535	before invoking the case function.
536	+
537	The subshell boundary ensures that variable mutations, function redefinitions, and side effects in one case cannot leak to another.
538	
539	[[busx_temp_dir]]
540	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
541	{busx_temp_dir}::
542	Per-case temporary directory (`BUT_TEMP_DIR`), set by
543	{busx_tcase}.
544	Created as a subdirectory of the fixture's root temp dir, named after the case function.
545	
546	[[busx_burv_root]]
547	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
548	{busx_burv_root}::
549	Per-case BURV isolation root (`BUTE_BURV_ROOT`), set by
550	{busx_tcase}.
551	When present, all invocation tiers create per-invoke BURV directories under this root, enabling multi-step test sequences to produce and consume isolated output.
552	
553	=== Fact-File Pattern
554	
555	[[busff_fact_file]]
556	//axl_voices axo_entity
557	{busff_fact_file}::
558	Inter-step communication pattern using named output files.
559	+
560	A producer writes a value to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${CONSTANT}` during command execution.
561	A consumer reads it from `${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${CONSTANT}` after invocation.
562	The kindle constant name is the contract between producer and consumer.
563	+
564	Example:
565	+
566	[source,bash]
567	----
568	# Producer (in command implementation):
569	echo "${z_hallmark}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}"
570	
571	# Consumer (in test case, after buto_tt_expect_ok):
572	z_hallmark=$(<"${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}")
573	----
574	+
575	The `current/` path segment maps
576	{burd_output_dir}
577	inside the BURV isolation tree.
578	Fact-file constants are defined as `readonly` in kindle (e.g., `RBF_FACT_HALLMARK`).
579	
580	[[busff_multi_fact]]
581	//axl_voices axo_entity
582	{busff_multi_fact}::
583	Inter-step communication pattern for emitting multiple facts of a uniform shape during a single dispatch.
584	Each fact lands as a file named `<root>.<extension>` under
585	{burd_output_dir}
586	(and durably under
587	{burd_temp_dir}),
588	where the extension classifies the fact type and the root identifies the per-row instance.
589	+
590	The pattern composes three rules:
591	+
592	* *Type-as-extension naming.* The extension token carries the fact's classification; the root carries the row identity.
593	A producer emitting per-alias observation facts writes `<alias>.buf_ext_alias` rather than encoding the type into the filename stem.
594	* *Closed registry.* Each allowed extension is a named constant of the shape `BUF_EXT_<NAME>` declared in `buf_fact.sh`.
595	Constant values match constant names downcased (e.g., `BUF_EXT_ALIAS="buf_ext_alias"`), so any fact file is recognizable on disk by extension alone.
596	Adding an extension is a deliberate edit to the registry; opaque ad-hoc extensions are not the shape.
597	* *BUK/RB boundary discipline.* The BUK registry holds only BUK-domain extensions.
598	RB-domain extensions register in `rbgc_Constants.sh` under the `RBGP_FACT_EXT_*` family, and neither universe enumerates the other.
599	Boundary is by ownership of the producer module, not by where the fact lands on disk.
600	+
601	Emission cardinality: one write per `<root, extension>` key per dispatch.
602	A second write of the same key during one dispatch is a bug — the implementing primitive
603	{buf_write_fact_multi}
604	fails fatally on preexistence to surface it.
605	+
606	Semantically distinct from
607	{busff_fact_file}:
608	single-form is one named-content slot per dispatch (the filename is the contract); multi-form is many same-shaped emissions per dispatch (the extension is the contract, the root is per-row).
609	
610	[[buf_write_fact_single]]
611	//axl_voices axo_entity
612	{buf_write_fact_single}::
613	Dual-write fact-file primitive — single-form.
614	Writes a named file to both
615	{burd_output_dir}
616	and
617	{burd_temp_dir}.
618	Dies if either copy already exists — a preexisting file indicates a double-write bug.
619	+
620	{burd_output_dir}
621	is the latest-command convenience copy, cleared on next dispatch.
622	{burd_temp_dir}
623	is the durable copy, addressable by anyone who captured the path.
624	+
625	All single-form fact-file producers must use
626	{buf_write_fact_single}
627	to ensure durable copies exist; multi-row producers use
628	{buf_write_fact_multi}.
629	+
630	[source,bash]
631	----
632	# Single-value fact:
633	buf_write_fact_single "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"
634	
635	# Single-slot fact whose body has structured content (e.g., burx.env):
636	buf_write_fact_single "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
637	----
638	
639	[[buf_write_fact_multi]]
640	//axl_voices axo_entity
641	{buf_write_fact_multi}::
642	Dual-write fact-file primitive — multi-form, implementing the
643	{busff_multi_fact}
644	pattern.
645	Writes a value to `<file_root>.<ext>` in both
646	{burd_output_dir}
647	and
648	{burd_temp_dir}.
649	Dies if either copy already exists.
650	+
651	Signature: `buf_write_fact_multi <file_root> <ext> <value>`.
652	The extension is an opaque pass-through — the function performs no registry validation; the registered `BUF_EXT_*` constant at the call site is the contract, and out-of-registry usage is a producer-side bug to be caught by code review, not by this primitive.
653	+
654	[source,bash]
655	----
656	# Per-row emission inside a producer loop:
657	for z_alias in "${z_aliases[@]}"; do
658	  buf_write_fact_multi "${z_alias}" "${BUF_EXT_ALIAS}" ""
659	done
660	----
661	+
662	Mirror of
663	{buf_write_fact_single}
664	for the multi-row shape; both use the same dual-write + fail-on-preexist semantics.
665	
666	[[buf_burx_env]]
667	//axpof_fact
668	{buf_burx_env}::
669	Tinder constant naming the
670	{burx_regime}
671	fact-file (`burx.env`).
672	Written by bud_dispatch.sh via
673	{buf_write_fact_single}
674	at dispatch start, with terminal fields appended at completion.
675	
676	[[buf_ext_alias]]
677	//axl_voices axt_enum_value
678	{buf_ext_alias}::
679	Registered BUK-domain extension constant for BURN alias observation facts (`BUF_EXT_ALIAS="buf_ext_alias"`).
680	Written by `burn_list_capture` (in `burn_regime.sh`) once per discovered alias profile, with empty content — presence-of-file is the fact, not file content.
681	+
682	First and currently sole member of the
683	{busff_multi_fact}
684	registry; serves as the proof-of-shape for the convention.
685	
686	=== Yelp Pattern
687	
688	[[busy_yelp]]
689	//axl_voices axk_premise
690	{busy_yelp}::
691	A formatted string fragment for interpolation into `buh_line` calls.
692	Yelp content **must not contain literal `%` characters** — this premise allows safe interpolation into `printf` format strings via bash variable expansion.
693	+
694	Produced by
695	{busy_yawp_s}
696	via pure assignment of inert
697	{busy_diastema}
698	markers, resolved at display time by
699	{busy_format_yawp}.
700	+
701	Yelps are the vocabulary-first primitive for handbook output.
702	A kit defines vocabulary constants at kindle time by capturing yelps into readonly globals (e.g., `RBYC_DEPOT`, `RBYC_HALLMARK`).
703	Call sites compose lines by interpolating these constants into `buh_line` strings.
704	
705	[[busy_yawp]]
706	//axl_voices axo_entity
707	{busy_yawp}::
708	A `buyy_*_yawp` function in `buym_yelp.sh` that produces a
709	{busy_yelp}
710	via pure bash assignment into the group return variable `z_buym_yelp`.
711	No subshells, no stdout, no process spawning — cannot fail.
712	+
713	Yawp functions stamp inert
714	{busy_diastema}
715	markers into strings.
716	All terminal capability decisions are deferred to
717	{busy_format_yawp}
718	at display time.
719	Eight yawp functions exist: `buyy_cmd_yawp` (command), `buyy_ui_yawp` (UI element), `buyy_href_yawp` (web hyperlink), `buyy_link_yawp` (vocabulary link), `buyy_tt_yawp` (resolved tabtarget), `buyy_pass_yawp` (pass/green), `buyy_warn_yawp` (warn/orange), `buyy_fail_yawp` (fail/red).
720	
721	[[busy_diastema]]
722	//axl_voices axo_entity
723	{busy_diastema}::
724	A non-printing byte sequence (`\x02\x1N`) embedded in a
725	{busy_yelp}
726	by a
727	{busy_yawp}
728	to delimit styled regions.
729	Eight markers form the diastema family: CMD, UI, HREF_URL, HREF_TEXT, LINK_URL, LINK_TEXT, TT, and END.
730	Openers identify the region type; END returns to the ambient color.
731	HREF and LINK have separate markers because vocabulary links (teaching terms) and web links (external references) serve different pedagogical purposes and carry independent palette slots.
732	
733	[[busy_format_yawp]]
734	//axl_voices axo_entity
735	{busy_format_yawp}::
736	The single intelligence point for yelp display: `buyf_format_yawp(color, string)`.
737	Takes a `BUYC_*` ambient color constant and a
738	{busy_diastema}-marked
739	string, resolves all markers into ANSI color sequences and OSC-8 hyperlinks, and sets `z_buym_format`.
740	+
741	Resolution order: structured markers (HREF, LINK) via BASH_REMATCH extraction, then simple markers (CMD, UI, TT) via string replacement, then END markers replaced with the ambient color.
742	
743	[[busy_configurator]]
744	//axl_voices axo_entity
745	{busy_configurator}::
746	A `buyc_*` function called before kindle to set the formatting mode flag.
747	Three configurators exist: `buyc_dispatch` (probe TERM/NO_COLOR/BURD_*), `buyc_unconditional` (always color), `buyc_plain` (never color).
748	Kindle reads the mode flag and defines the readonly `BUYC_*` public color palette and hyperlink mode.
749

892	=== BURN Regime
893	
894	[[burn_regime]]
895	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
896	{burn_regime}::
897	Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote nodes.
898	Unlike singleton regimes, BURN is multi-instance: one assignment file per SSH connection,
899	stored at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/<alias>/burn.env`.
900	Each profile describes one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point —
901	classified by {burn_tier} as either privileged (node-level operations) or workload
902	(user-level operations on an established node).
903	
904	[[burn_host]]
905	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
906	{burn_host}::
907	IP address or hostname of the remote node.
908	
909	[[burn_user]]
910	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
911	{burn_user}::
912	Username on the remote node.
913	May differ from the local
914	{burs_user}.
915	
916	[[burn_alias]]
917	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
918	{burn_alias}::
919	SSH alias and key filename.
920	Must match the directory name containing this profile's assignment file.
921	
922	[[burn_ssh_pubkey]]
923	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
924	{burn_ssh_pubkey}::
925	Full SSH public key line (e.g., `ssh-ed25519 AAAA... user@host`).
926	Git-transported: no private key material.
927	Used by handbook procedures to render `authorized_keys` entries and `ssh-keygen` commands.
928	
929	[[burn_key_file]]
930	//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
931	{burn_key_file}::
932	SSH private key filename within `~/.ssh/`.
933	Empty defaults to {burn_alias} (one key per connection).
934	Localhost profiles set `id_ed25519` to share the invoking user's default key.
935	
936	[[burn_command]]
937	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
938	{burn_command}::
939	Shell command for `command=` routing in SSH `authorized_keys`.
940	Empty for direct-shell connections (Linux, macOS, localhost — no routing needed).
941	When set, determines which shell or environment the connection launches
942	(e.g., `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` for Cygwin, `wsl.exe -d Ubuntu` for WSL).
943	
944	[[burn_tier]]
945	//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
946	{burn_tier}::
947	Privilege classification of this profile.
948	Values: `privileged` (admin-level operations on the node — sshd configuration,
949	user provisioning, node-level setup) or `workload` (normal remote work as an
950	ephemeral user on an established node).
951	Tabtargets enforce tier preconditions at entry: privileged-only operations
952	refuse workload aliases and vice versa.
953

1058	
1059	[[burd_log_hist]]
1060	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
1061	{burd_log_hist}::
1062	Path to the timestamped historical log file.
1063	
1064	==== Parsed Variables
1065	
1066	[[burd_group_parsed]]
1067	//axl_voices axhrgb_group
1068	{burd_group_parsed}::
1069	Variables extracted from the tabtarget filename during argument processing.
1070	
1071	[[burd_command]]
1072	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1073	{burd_command}::
1074	Primary command token (first token from tabtarget split).
1075	
1076	[[burd_target]]
1077	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1078	{burd_target}::
1079	Complete tabtarget string as received by dispatch.
1080	
1081	[[burd_cli_args]]
1082	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_repeated
1083	{burd_cli_args}::
1084	Array of additional CLI arguments passed after the tabtarget.
1085	
1086	[[burd_token_1]]
1087	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1088	{burd_token_1}::
1089	Positional token 1 from tabtarget split (the colophon).
1090	
1091	[[burd_token_2]]
1092	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1093	{burd_token_2}::
1094	Positional token 2 from tabtarget split (the frontispiece).
1095	
1096	[[burd_token_3]]
1097	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1098	{burd_token_3}::
1099	Positional token 3 from tabtarget split (the imprint, if present).
1100	
1101	[[burd_token_4]]
1102	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1103	{burd_token_4}::
1104	Positional token 4 from tabtarget split.
1105	
1106	[[burd_token_5]]
1107	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1108	{burd_token_5}::
1109	Positional token 5 from tabtarget split.
1110	
1111	=== BURE Regime
1112	
1113	[[bure_regime]]
1114	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
1115	{bure_regime}::
1116	Ambient regime for caller-supplied behavioral overrides to BUK primitives.
1117	Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURE variables are set directly in the caller's environment.
1118	Kindled and validated when buc_command.sh is sourced.
1119	
1120	[[bure_countdown]]
1121	//axvr_variable axtu_string axd_optional
1122	{bure_countdown}::
1123	Countdown behavior override.
1124	When set to `skip`, buc_countdown bypasses interactive countdown and tty output.
1125	When unset, countdown proceeds normally with interactive tty cancel window.
1126	Any other value fails validation.
1127	
1128	[[bure_verbose]]
1129	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
1130	{bure_verbose}::
1131	Verbosity level controlling diagnostic output.
1132	0=silent, 1=show messages, 2=enable bash trace.
1133	
1134	[[bure_color]]
1135	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1136	{bure_color}::
1137	Color output policy.
1138	Input as auto/0/1; resolved to 0 or 1 during dispatch.
1139	Respects NO_COLOR environment convention.
1140	
1141	==== Tweak Mechanism
1142	
1143	The tweak mechanism provides a single caller-supplied name/value override that flows through invocation without BUK interpreting its semantics.
1144	BUK enrolls, validates, and propagates the pair; consuming kits (e.g., RBK) check the name and act on the value.
1145	
1146	Constraint: one tweak per invocation.
1147	The caller sets at most one
1148	{bure_tweak_name}/{bure_tweak_value}
1149	pair in the environment before invoking a tabtarget.
1150	The pair is ambient — it propagates through `zbuto_invoke` like all BURE variables, without requiring re-kindle in the invoked command.
1151	
1152	[[bure_tweak_name]]
1153	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
1154	{bure_tweak_name}::
1155	Consumer-interpreted tweak identifier.
1156	The name selects which behavioral override the consuming kit applies.
1157	BUK validates length (0–64 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
1158	+
1159	Example: `graft_image` — tells RBK to substitute the graft source image.
1160	
1161	[[bure_tweak_value]]
1162	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
1163	{bure_tweak_value}::
1164	Consumer-interpreted tweak payload.
1165	The value is opaque to BUK; its format and interpretation are defined by whatever kit checks
1166	{bure_tweak_name}.
1167	BUK validates length (0–256 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
1168	+
1169	Either variable may be set independently — BUK does not enforce pairing.
1170	Consumer kits that require both must enforce that themselves.
1171	
1172	==== Exchange Labels
1173	
1174	[[bure_label]]
1175	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_xname axd_optional
1176	{bure_label}::
1177	Correlation label for cross-project exchange.
1178	When set, propagated verbatim into
1179	{burx_label}
1180	during dispatch.
1181	When unset, BUD writes empty string.
1182	Maximum 120 characters, xname format.
1183	+
1184	BUD writes whatever
1185	{bure_label}
1186	contains without interpreting the semantics.
1187	
1188	=== BURX Regime
1189	
1190	[[burx_prefix]]
1191	//axl_voices axrg_prefix
1192	{burx_prefix}::
1193	Bash Utility Exchange Regime
1194	
1195	[[burx_regime]]
1196	//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
1197	{burx_regime}::
1198	Exchange regime for cross-project dispatch state.
1199	Written by bud_dispatch.sh to both
1200	{burd_output_dir}
1201	and
1202	{burd_temp_dir}
1203	as `burx.env` via
1204	{buf_write_fact_single}.
1205	Unlike single-project regimes,
1206	{burx_regime}
1207	is designed to be read across a remote project boundary.
1208	+
1209	Variables appear in two phases:
1210	initial fields are written at dispatch start;
1211	terminal fields are appended at dispatch completion.
1212	Absence of
1213	{burx_exit_status}
1214	indicates the dispatch is still running.
1215	
1216	[[burx_pid]]
1217	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
1218	{burx_pid}::
1219	Process ID of the dispatched command.
1220	
1221	[[burx_began_at]]
1222	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1223	{burx_began_at}::
1224	Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch started.
1225	Format: `YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.NNNNNNNNN`.
1226	
1227	[[burx_tabtarget]]
1228	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
1229	{burx_tabtarget}::
1230	The tabtarget that was dispatched.
1231	Corresponds to
1232	{burd_target}.
1233	
1234	[[burx_temp_dir]]
1235	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
1236	{burx_temp_dir}::
1237	Absolute path to the dispatch temporary directory.
1238	Corresponds to
1239	{burd_temp_dir}.
1240	
1241	[[burx_transcript]]
1242	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
1243	{burx_transcript}::
1244	Absolute path to the transcript file.
1245	Corresponds to
1246	{burd_transcript}.
1247	
1248	[[burx_log_hist]]
1249	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
1250	{burx_log_hist}::
1251	Absolute path to the historical log file.
1252	Corresponds to
1253	{burd_log_hist}.
1254	
1255	[[burx_label]]
1256	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
1257	{burx_label}::
1258	Correlation label from
1259	{bure_label}.
1260	Always present — empty string when
1261	{bure_label}
1262	is not set.
1263	Format and interpretation are opaque to BUD.
1264	
1265	[[burx_exit_status]]
1266	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer axd_optional
1267	{burx_exit_status}::
1268	Unix exit code of the dispatched command.
1269	Absence indicates the dispatch is still running.
1270	
1271	[[burx_ended_at]]
1272	//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
1273	{burx_ended_at}::
1274	Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch completed.
1275	Same format as
1276	{burx_began_at}.
1277	Present only when
1278	{burx_exit_status}
1279	is present.
1280	
1281	include::BUSD-DispatchRuntime.adoc[]
1282	
1283	== Remote Node Access
1284	
1285	Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
1286	trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
1287	workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.
1288	
1289	=== Privilege Tiers
1290	
1291	The {burn_tier} field of every {burn_regime} profile classifies the profile
1292	as either `privileged` or `workload`. Tabtargets refuse aliases of the wrong
1293	tier at entry.
1294	
1295	==== Privileged Tier
1296	
1297	Privileged profiles authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or equivalent
1298	privileges. Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user
1299	provisioning, node-establishment ceremonies.
1300	
1301	Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
1302	format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
1303	station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
1304	enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.
1305	
1306	==== Workload Tier
1307	
1308	Workload profiles authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
1309	privileged authority. Used for normal remote work: running commands,
1310	file operations, interactive shells.
1311	
1312	Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
1313	station users. Lifecycle is short:
1314	{busn_conscript}
1315	when needed,
1316	{busn_discharge}
1317	when done.
1318	
1319	=== Tabtarget Catalog
1320	
1321	Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
1322	operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.
1323	
1324	==== Lifecycle
1325	
1326	`buw-rnl`::
1327	List all {burn_regime} profiles in the local station subtree.
1328	
1329	`buw-rnr <alias>`::
1330	Render a single {burn_regime} profile.
1331	
1332	`buw-rnv <alias>`::
1333	Validate a single {burn_regime} profile.
1334	
1335	==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)
1336	
1337	`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x} <host> <user> <moniker>`::
1338	{busn_garrison} a node for the named platform.
1339	Writes the privileged {burn_regime} profile and runs the platform-specific
1340	ceremony to establish admin trust.
1341	Platform letters: l=Linux, m=Mac, c=Cygwin, w=WSL, p=PowerShell, x=Localhost.
1342	
1343	==== Workload (privileged authority required)
1344	
1345	`buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x} <privileged-alias> <workload-moniker>`::
1346	{busn_conscript} a workload user via the named privileged BURN.
1347	Creates the remote OS user, installs the station user's pubkey, and
1348	writes a workload {burn_regime} profile locally.
1349	
1350	`buw-nwd <workload-alias>`::
1351	{busn_discharge} a workload user.
1352	Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
1353	authority and removes the local profile.
1354	
1355	`buw-nwi <privileged-alias>`::
1356	{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
1357	privileged BURN.
1358	
1359	==== Workload (operational)
1360	
1361	`buw-nwc <workload-alias>`::
1362	Check responsiveness via passwordless SSH probe.
1363	
1364	`buw-nwr <workload-alias> <cmd-file>`::
1365	Run the contents of `<cmd-file>` on the remote node; capture output to
1366	{burd_output_dir}.
1367	
1368	`buw-nws <workload-alias>`::
1369	Open an interactive SSH session.
1370	
1371	==== Handbook
1372	
1373	`buw-hn0`::
1374	Top-level handbook landing for node access procedures.
1375	
1376	=== Privileges
1377	
1378	The {burs_user} owns its profile subtree at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/`
1379	exclusively — operations write only under the current station user's tree.
1380	
1381	A {burn_regime} profile describes a connection identity (host, user, shell,
1382	alias, key, tier) — not project state, not work-directory. Project context
1383	is supplied by the consumer at invocation time.
1384	
1385	Each platform subdoc declares the remote-user privilege contract that its
1386	ceremony assumes (e.g., on Windows the privileged user must be a member of
1387	the Administrators group).
1388	
1389	=== Verb Definitions
1390	
1391	[[busn_garrison]]
1392	//axl_voices axt_command
1393	{busn_garrison}::
1394	Establish privileged BURN trust on a node by writing the privileged
1395	{burn_regime} profile and running the platform-specific ceremony to gain
1396	admin access. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-npg{platform}`. First-time
1397	node setup; idempotent on re-run.
1398	
1399	[[busn_conscript]]
1400	//axl_voices axt_command
1401	{busn_conscript}::
1402	Create an ephemeral workload user on a node and provision a workload
1403	{burn_regime} profile locally. Requires a privileged BURN as authority for
1404	the remote-user creation. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-nwc{platform}`,
1405	where the platform letter selects the workload shell — independent of the
1406	privileged BURN's shell choice.
1407	
1408	[[busn_discharge]]
1409	//axl_voices axt_command
1410	{busn_discharge}::
1411	Remove a workload user from a node and delete the local workload
1412	{burn_regime} profile. The workload profile encodes its privileged
1413	authority; {busn_discharge} uses that authority to delete the remote OS
1414	user. Tabtarget: `buw-nwd`.
1415	
1416	[[busn_inventory]]
1417	//axl_voices axt_command
1418	{busn_inventory}::
1419	List workload users currently provisioned on a node, queried via a
1420	privileged BURN. Scope is the node's full workload-user population — not
1421	just the current station user's history. Tabtarget: `buw-nwi`.
1422	
1423	include::BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc[]
1424	
1425	include::BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc[]
1426	
1427	include::BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc[]
1428

= Bash Utilities Specification
:doctype: book

// tag::mapping-section[]
//
// Category declarations:
// bust_:  Tabtarget (launcher naming vesture)
// busd:   Dispatch (non-terminal, tabtarget system umbrella)
// busdc_: Dispatch Colophon (routing identifier)
// busdf_: Dispatch Frontispiece (human-readable description)
// busdi_: Dispatch Imprint (embedded parameter/target specifier)
// busdm_: Dispatch Formulary (routing component)
// busdl_: Dispatch Launcher (bootstrap script)
// bukro_: Kit Regime Operation (kindle/validate/render)
// burc_:  Configuration Regime (project-level settings)
// burn_:  Node Regime (per-node SSH connection profiles, multi-instance)
// burs_:  Station Regime (developer-level settings)
// burd_:  Dispatch Runtime (ephemeral state assembled during dispatch)
// bure_:  Environment Regime (ambient caller-supplied behavioral overrides)
// busi:   Invocation (test invocation umbrella)
// busn_:  Node Access (verbs for remote node management)
// busit_: Invocation Tier (tier classification)
// busig_: Invocation Global (capture state set by zbuto_invoke)
// busx_:  Case Boundary (test case isolation runner)
// busff_: Fact-File (inter-step communication via output files)
// buf_:   Fact Module (dual-write fact-file primitives)
// busy_:  Yelp (pre-rendered formatted string capture)
// burx_:  Exchange Regime (cross-project dispatch state)
//
// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//

// Tabtarget Vesture
:bust_tabtarget:        <<bust_tabtarget,Tabtarget Vesture>>

// Dispatch
:busdc_colophon:        <<busdc_colophon,Colophon>>
:busdc_colophon_s:      <<busdc_colophon,Colophons>>
:busdf_frontispiece:    <<busdf_frontispiece,Frontispiece>>
:busdf_frontispiece_s:  <<busdf_frontispiece,Frontispieces>>

:busdi_imprint_s:       <<busdi_imprint,Imprints>>

:busdl_launcher:        <<busdl_launcher,Launcher>>
:busdl_launcher_s:      <<busdl_launcher,Launchers>>

// Axial Regime Terms (from AXLA — matching RBSA)

:axrg_regime:                      <<axrg_regime,Regime>>

// BURD Parsed Variables

// BURE Regime: Environment (ambient caller-supplied behavioral overrides)

// Invocation Tier
:busit_unit:                      <<busit_unit,Unit Tier>>

:busit_launch:                    <<busit_launch,Launch Tier>>

// Invocation Globals

// Case Boundary
:busx_tcase:                      <<busx_tcase,Case Boundary>>

:busx_burv_root:                  <<busx_burv_root,BUTE_BURV_ROOT>>

// Fact-File
:busff_fact_file:                 <<busff_fact_file,Fact-File>>
:busff_multi_fact:                <<busff_multi_fact,Multi-Element Fact-File>>

// Fact Module
:buf_write_fact_single:           <<buf_write_fact_single,buf_write_fact_single>>
:buf_write_fact_multi:            <<buf_write_fact_multi,buf_write_fact_multi>>
:buf_burx_env:                    <<buf_burx_env,BUF_burx_env>>

:busy_yelp:                       <<busy_yelp,Yelp>>
:busy_yelp_s:                     <<busy_yelp,Yelps>>
:busy_yawp:                       <<busy_yawp,Yawp Function>>
:busy_yawp_s:                     <<busy_yawp,Yawp Functions>>
:busy_diastema:                   <<busy_diastema,Diastema>>
:busy_diastema_s:                 <<busy_diastema,Diastemata>>
:busy_format_yawp:                <<busy_format_yawp,Format Yawp>>

// BURX Regime: Exchange (cross-project dispatch state)

== Overview

The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.

BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.

For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.

== Tabtarget Vesture

[[bust_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{bust_tabtarget}::
Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
+
* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
* separator: `.`
* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
* envelope: `.sh`
+
Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation

== Dispatch

The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
A
{busdl_launcher}
validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
The
{busdc_colophon}
identifies the routing path;
the
{busdf_frontispiece}
provides human-readable description;
the
{busdi_imprint}
embeds target parameters.

[[busdc_colophon]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdc_colophon}::
The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdc_colophon}
determines which
{busdm_formulary}
handles the dispatch.
Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
+
The
{busdc_colophon}
is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench

[[busdf_frontispiece]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdf_frontispiece}::
The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdf_frontispiece}
uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
+
Appears after the
{busdc_colophon}
and dot separator.
+
Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.

[[busdi_imprint]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdi_imprint}::
An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
An
{busdi_imprint}
specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
+
Appears after a second dot separator following the
{busdf_frontispiece}.
+
Examples:
+
* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`

[[busdm_formulary]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdm_formulary}::
A component that routes
{busdc_colophon_s}
to implementations.
Each
{busdm_formulary}
registers handlers for a set of
{busdc_colophon_s}
and dispatches to the appropriate function.
+
Types:
+
* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
+
A
{busdm_formulary}
is sourced by the
{busdl_launcher}
and provides the dispatch function.

[[busdl_launcher]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdl_launcher}::
A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
A
{busdl_launcher}
is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
+
Naming follows
{bust_tabtarget}:
`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Responsibilities:
+
* Parse
{busdc_colophon}
from own filename
* Validate required arguments
* Source the target
{busdm_formulary}
* Invoke the dispatched function

== Routing Behavior

=== Dispatch Flow

. User invokes launcher: `./tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
. Launcher parses its filename to extract:
* {busdc_colophon}:
`rbw-cr`
* {busdf_frontispiece}:
`Rack`
* {busdi_imprint}:
`tadmor`
. Launcher sources the appropriate
{busdm_formulary}
(`rbw_workbench.sh`)
. Formulary's dispatch function routes based on frontispiece
. Target function executes with imprint as parameter

=== Workbench Pattern

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}w_workbench.sh

{prefix}w_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    local imprint="$2"
    shift 2

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Build)          {prefix}w_build "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        Rack)           {prefix}w_rack "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        *)              buc_fatal "Unknown frontispiece: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Testbench Pattern

Testbenches follow the same pattern but route to test functions:

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}t_testbench.sh

{prefix}t_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    shift

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Unit)        {prefix}t_unit "$@" ;;
        Integration) {prefix}t_integration "$@" ;;
        *)           buc_fatal "Unknown test: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Test Registry Model

The test registry (`butr_registry.sh`) uses two orthogonal enrollment dimensions:

**Fixture** — init/setup execution context.
Each test case belongs to exactly one fixture.
Fixtures provide isolation via `_tsuite` subshell boundaries.
Registered with `butr_fixture_enroll(name, init_fn, setup_fn)`.

**Suite** — cross-cutting selection group (N:M with cases).
A case can belong to multiple suites.
Suites classify by dependency tier (what environment must exist), not by test methodology.
A suite is a membership tag.
Suites enable suite dispatch: run a cross-cutting subset of cases grouped by their owning fixtures.
Suite context is set with `butr_suite_enroll(SUITE1 [SUITE2...])` before case enrollments.
Queried by `butd_run_suite(suite_name)`.

Standard suite constants (defined as `readonly` in testbench kindle):

* `BUTR_SUITE_FAST` — no external dependencies, CI-safe
* `BUTR_SUITE_SERVICE` — needs credentials, no container runtime
* `BUTR_SUITE_COMPLETE` — full test matrix including container runtime

Dispatch entry points:

* `butd_run_all()` — all fixtures, all cases
* `butd_run_fixture(name [case...])` — single fixture, optional case filter
* `butd_run_one(fn)` — single case with its fixture setup
* `butd_run_suite(suite)` — cross-cutting suite, grouped by fixture

=== Colophon Conventions

{busdc_colophon_s}
follow a structure that encodes both kit identity and formulary type:

* `{cipher}-` — default workbench (e.g., `rbw-`, `jjw-`)
* `{cipher}-t-` — testbench (e.g., `buw-t-`, `jjw-t-`)
* `{cipher}-{variant}-` — specialized workbench (e.g., `rbw-cr-` for crucible rack operations)

The trailing hyphen is always part of the colophon — it separates the routing prefix from the dot-delimited tabtarget components.

== Test Invocation

The test invocation vocabulary defines how test cases exercise code under controlled capture.
All invocation tiers share a common primitive,
`zbuto_invoke`,
which captures stdout, stderr, exit status, and optional BURV output into
{busig_stdout},
{busig_stderr},
{busig_status},
and
{busig_burv_output}.

=== Invocation Tiers

Three tiers provide increasing levels of resolution:

[[busit_unit]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_unit}::
Invokes a raw function or command directly via `zbuto_invoke`.
No tabtarget file or workbench routing involved.
+
API: `buto_unit_expect_ok(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_fatal(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_ok_stdout(expected, command...)`

[[busit_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_tabtarget}::
Resolves a
{busdc_colophon}
to a
{busdl_launcher}
file in `tt/`, then invokes it via `zbuto_invoke`.
Dies if no tabtarget matches or if multiple matches exist.
+
API: `buto_tt_expect_ok(colophon, args...)`, `buto_tt_expect_fatal(colophon, args...)`

[[busit_launch]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_launch}::
Invokes a workbench dispatch function directly, bypassing the
{busdl_launcher}
file.
Useful when exercising workbench routing without a tabtarget on disk.
+
API: `buto_launch_expect_ok(launcher, colophon, args...)`, `buto_launch_expect_fatal(launcher, colophon, args...)`

=== Capture Globals

Every call to `zbuto_invoke` sets four globals that subsequent assertions can inspect.

[[busig_stdout]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{busig_stdout}::
Captured stdout from the invoked command.

[[busig_stderr]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{busig_stderr}::
Captured stderr from the invoked command.

[[busig_status]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{busig_status}::
Exit code from the invoked command.

[[busig_burv_output]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busig_burv_output}::
BURV output directory for the invocation.
Empty when
{busx_burv_root}
is not set.
When set, contains the invocation's isolated output tree rooted at
`{BUTE_BURV_ROOT}/invoke-NNNNN/output`.

=== Case Boundary

[[busx_tcase]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busx_tcase}::
The case isolation runner (`zbute_tcase`).
Executes a single test case function in a subshell with per-case temporary state.
Sets
{busx_temp_dir}
and
{busx_burv_root}
before invoking the case function.
+
The subshell boundary ensures that variable mutations, function redefinitions, and side effects in one case cannot leak to another.

[[busx_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busx_temp_dir}::
Per-case temporary directory (`BUT_TEMP_DIR`), set by
{busx_tcase}.
Created as a subdirectory of the fixture's root temp dir, named after the case function.

[[busx_burv_root]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busx_burv_root}::
Per-case BURV isolation root (`BUTE_BURV_ROOT`), set by
{busx_tcase}.
When present, all invocation tiers create per-invoke BURV directories under this root, enabling multi-step test sequences to produce and consume isolated output.

=== Fact-File Pattern

[[busff_fact_file]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busff_fact_file}::
Inter-step communication pattern using named output files.
+
A producer writes a value to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${CONSTANT}` during command execution.
A consumer reads it from `${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${CONSTANT}` after invocation.
The kindle constant name is the contract between producer and consumer.
+
Example:
+
[source,bash]
----
# Producer (in command implementation):
echo "${z_hallmark}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}"

# Consumer (in test case, after buto_tt_expect_ok):
z_hallmark=$(<"${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}")
----
+
The `current/` path segment maps
{burd_output_dir}
inside the BURV isolation tree.
Fact-file constants are defined as `readonly` in kindle (e.g., `RBF_FACT_HALLMARK`).

[[busff_multi_fact]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busff_multi_fact}::
Inter-step communication pattern for emitting multiple facts of a uniform shape during a single dispatch.
Each fact lands as a file named `<root>.<extension>` under
{burd_output_dir}
(and durably under
{burd_temp_dir}),
where the extension classifies the fact type and the root identifies the per-row instance.
+
The pattern composes three rules:
+
* *Type-as-extension naming.* The extension token carries the fact's classification; the root carries the row identity.
A producer emitting per-alias observation facts writes `<alias>.buf_ext_alias` rather than encoding the type into the filename stem.
* *Closed registry.* Each allowed extension is a named constant of the shape `BUF_EXT_<NAME>` declared in `buf_fact.sh`.
Constant values match constant names downcased (e.g., `BUF_EXT_ALIAS="buf_ext_alias"`), so any fact file is recognizable on disk by extension alone.
Adding an extension is a deliberate edit to the registry; opaque ad-hoc extensions are not the shape.
* *BUK/RB boundary discipline.* The BUK registry holds only BUK-domain extensions.
RB-domain extensions register in `rbgc_Constants.sh` under the `RBGP_FACT_EXT_*` family, and neither universe enumerates the other.
Boundary is by ownership of the producer module, not by where the fact lands on disk.
+
Emission cardinality: one write per `<root, extension>` key per dispatch.
A second write of the same key during one dispatch is a bug — the implementing primitive
{buf_write_fact_multi}
fails fatally on preexistence to surface it.
+
Semantically distinct from
{busff_fact_file}:
single-form is one named-content slot per dispatch (the filename is the contract); multi-form is many same-shaped emissions per dispatch (the extension is the contract, the root is per-row).

[[buf_write_fact_single]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{buf_write_fact_single}::
Dual-write fact-file primitive — single-form.
Writes a named file to both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}.
Dies if either copy already exists — a preexisting file indicates a double-write bug.
+
{burd_output_dir}
is the latest-command convenience copy, cleared on next dispatch.
{burd_temp_dir}
is the durable copy, addressable by anyone who captured the path.
+
All single-form fact-file producers must use
{buf_write_fact_single}
to ensure durable copies exist; multi-row producers use
{buf_write_fact_multi}.
+
[source,bash]
----
# Single-value fact:
buf_write_fact_single "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"

# Single-slot fact whose body has structured content (e.g., burx.env):
buf_write_fact_single "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
----

[[buf_write_fact_multi]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{buf_write_fact_multi}::
Dual-write fact-file primitive — multi-form, implementing the
{busff_multi_fact}
pattern.
Writes a value to `<file_root>.<ext>` in both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}.
Dies if either copy already exists.
+
Signature: `buf_write_fact_multi <file_root> <ext> <value>`.
The extension is an opaque pass-through — the function performs no registry validation; the registered `BUF_EXT_*` constant at the call site is the contract, and out-of-registry usage is a producer-side bug to be caught by code review, not by this primitive.
+
[source,bash]
----
# Per-row emission inside a producer loop:
for z_alias in "${z_aliases[@]}"; do
  buf_write_fact_multi "${z_alias}" "${BUF_EXT_ALIAS}" ""
done
----
+
Mirror of
{buf_write_fact_single}
for the multi-row shape; both use the same dual-write + fail-on-preexist semantics.

[[buf_burx_env]]
//axpof_fact
{buf_burx_env}::
Tinder constant naming the
{burx_regime}
fact-file (`burx.env`).
Written by bud_dispatch.sh via
{buf_write_fact_single}
at dispatch start, with terminal fields appended at completion.

[[buf_ext_alias]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value
{buf_ext_alias}::
Registered BUK-domain extension constant for BURN alias observation facts (`BUF_EXT_ALIAS="buf_ext_alias"`).
Written by `burn_list_capture` (in `burn_regime.sh`) once per discovered alias profile, with empty content — presence-of-file is the fact, not file content.
+
First and currently sole member of the
{busff_multi_fact}
registry; serves as the proof-of-shape for the convention.

=== Yelp Pattern

[[busy_yelp]]
//axl_voices axk_premise
{busy_yelp}::
A formatted string fragment for interpolation into `buh_line` calls.
Yelp content **must not contain literal `%` characters** — this premise allows safe interpolation into `printf` format strings via bash variable expansion.
+
Produced by
{busy_yawp_s}
via pure assignment of inert
{busy_diastema}
markers, resolved at display time by
{busy_format_yawp}.
+
Yelps are the vocabulary-first primitive for handbook output.
A kit defines vocabulary constants at kindle time by capturing yelps into readonly globals (e.g., `RBYC_DEPOT`, `RBYC_HALLMARK`).
Call sites compose lines by interpolating these constants into `buh_line` strings.

[[busy_yawp]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busy_yawp}::
A `buyy_*_yawp` function in `buym_yelp.sh` that produces a
{busy_yelp}
via pure bash assignment into the group return variable `z_buym_yelp`.
No subshells, no stdout, no process spawning — cannot fail.
+
Yawp functions stamp inert
{busy_diastema}
markers into strings.
All terminal capability decisions are deferred to
{busy_format_yawp}
at display time.
Eight yawp functions exist: `buyy_cmd_yawp` (command), `buyy_ui_yawp` (UI element), `buyy_href_yawp` (web hyperlink), `buyy_link_yawp` (vocabulary link), `buyy_tt_yawp` (resolved tabtarget), `buyy_pass_yawp` (pass/green), `buyy_warn_yawp` (warn/orange), `buyy_fail_yawp` (fail/red).

[[busy_diastema]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busy_diastema}::
A non-printing byte sequence (`\x02\x1N`) embedded in a
{busy_yelp}
by a
{busy_yawp}
to delimit styled regions.
Eight markers form the diastema family: CMD, UI, HREF_URL, HREF_TEXT, LINK_URL, LINK_TEXT, TT, and END.
Openers identify the region type; END returns to the ambient color.
HREF and LINK have separate markers because vocabulary links (teaching terms) and web links (external references) serve different pedagogical purposes and carry independent palette slots.

[[busy_format_yawp]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busy_format_yawp}::
The single intelligence point for yelp display: `buyf_format_yawp(color, string)`.
Takes a `BUYC_*` ambient color constant and a
{busy_diastema}-marked
string, resolves all markers into ANSI color sequences and OSC-8 hyperlinks, and sets `z_buym_format`.
+
Resolution order: structured markers (HREF, LINK) via BASH_REMATCH extraction, then simple markers (CMD, UI, TT) via string replacement, then END markers replaced with the ambient color.

[[busy_configurator]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busy_configurator}::
A `buyc_*` function called before kindle to set the formatting mode flag.
Three configurators exist: `buyc_dispatch` (probe TERM/NO_COLOR/BURD_*), `buyc_unconditional` (always color), `buyc_plain` (never color).
Kindle reads the mode flag and defines the readonly `BUYC_*` public color palette and hyperlink mode.

== Regime Configuration

BUK defines two
{axrg_regime_s}
for project and developer configuration.
{axrg_assignment_s}
use
{axf_bash}
format (shell variable assignment files that are sourced).

=== Regime Prefixes

[[burc_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burc_prefix}::
Bash Utility Configuration Regime

[[burs_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burs_prefix}::
Bash Utility Station Regime

[[burn_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burn_prefix}::
Bash Utility Regime Node

[[bure_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{bure_prefix}::
Bash Utility Environment Regime

=== Kit Regime Operations

[[bukro_kindle]]
//axvr_kindle
{bukro_kindle}::
Silent initialization of Bash Utility Kit regime state from
{axrg_assignment_s}.
Sets defaults, detects unexpected variables, builds derived structures.

[[bukro_validate]]
//axvr_validate
{bukro_validate}::
Strict field validation for Bash Utility Kit
{axrg_regime_s}.
Checks all type and constraint requirements via buv_env_* validators.

[[bukro_render]]
//axvr_render
{bukro_render}::
Diagnostic display of Bash Utility Kit
{axrg_regime}
state.

=== BURC Regime

[[burc_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced axrd_singleton
{burc_regime}::
Configuration regime for project-level BUK settings.
BURC assignment files in `.buk/burc.env` define repository structure and tabtarget infrastructure.

[[burc_station_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_station_file}::
Path to the developer's personal
{burs_regime}
assignment file.

[[burc_tabtarget_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_tabtarget_dir}::
Directory containing
{busdl_launcher}
scripts.

[[burc_tabtarget_delimiter]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burc_tabtarget_delimiter}::
Single-character token separator in
{bust_tabtarget}
inscriptions.

[[burc_tools_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_tools_dir}::
Directory containing BUK utilities and project tool scripts.

[[burc_project_root]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_project_root}::
Path from assignment file location to the project root directory.

[[burc_managed_kits]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burc_managed_kits}::
Comma-separated list of kits managed by vvx.
At release time, determines which kits are built into the binary.
At install time, must exactly match the parcel's kit list.

[[burc_temp_root_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_temp_root_dir}::
Root directory for BUK temporary files.

[[burc_output_root_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_output_root_dir}::
Root directory for BUK command output files.

[[burc_log_last]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burc_log_last}::
Filename stem for the most recent command execution log.

[[burc_log_ext]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burc_log_ext}::
File extension for log files, without leading dot.

=== BURS Regime

[[burs_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced axrd_singleton
{burs_regime}::
Configuration regime for developer-level BUK settings.
BURS assignment files are personal to each developer and not committed to version control.

[[burs_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burs_user}::
Local developer username.
Routes to the correct `.buk/users/` subdirectory for
{burn_regime}
profiles.

[[burs_log_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burs_log_dir}::
Directory where this developer stores BUK operation logs.

=== BURN Regime

[[burn_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
{burn_regime}::
Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote nodes.
Unlike singleton regimes, BURN is multi-instance: one assignment file per SSH connection,
stored at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/<alias>/burn.env`.
Each profile describes one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point —
classified by {burn_tier} as either privileged (node-level operations) or workload
(user-level operations on an established node).

[[burn_host]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_host}::
IP address or hostname of the remote node.

[[burn_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_user}::
Username on the remote node.
May differ from the local
{burs_user}.

[[burn_alias]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_alias}::
SSH alias and key filename.
Must match the directory name containing this profile's assignment file.

[[burn_ssh_pubkey]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_ssh_pubkey}::
Full SSH public key line (e.g., `ssh-ed25519 AAAA... user@host`).
Git-transported: no private key material.
Used by handbook procedures to render `authorized_keys` entries and `ssh-keygen` commands.

[[burn_key_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
{burn_key_file}::
SSH private key filename within `~/.ssh/`.
Empty defaults to {burn_alias} (one key per connection).
Localhost profiles set `id_ed25519` to share the invoking user's default key.

[[burn_command]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_command}::
Shell command for `command=` routing in SSH `authorized_keys`.
Empty for direct-shell connections (Linux, macOS, localhost — no routing needed).
When set, determines which shell or environment the connection launches
(e.g., `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` for Cygwin, `wsl.exe -d Ubuntu` for WSL).

[[burn_tier]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_tier}::
Privilege classification of this profile.
Values: `privileged` (admin-level operations on the node — sshd configuration,
user provisioning, node-level setup) or `workload` (normal remote work as an
ephemeral user on an established node).
Tabtargets enforce tier preconditions at entry: privileged-only operations
refuse workload aliases and vice versa.

=== BURD Regime

[[burd_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burd_prefix}::
Bash Utility Dispatch Runtime

[[burd_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{burd_regime}::
Ephemeral runtime state assembled by bud_dispatch.sh during tabtarget execution.
Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURD variables are constructed inline — no assignment file exists.
The dispatch main function IS the kindle: zbud_setup sources configuration and derives computed state, zbud_process_args parses the tabtarget into tokens.

==== Input Variables

[[burd_group_input]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_input}::
Variables set by the caller before dispatch invocation.

[[burd_regime_file]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_regime_file}::
Path to the BURC regime assignment file to source during setup.

[[burd_no_log]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burd_no_log}::
When non-empty, suppresses all log file creation and output.

[[burd_interactive]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burd_interactive}::
When non-empty, enables interactive mode with uncurated tee logging.

[[burd_coordinator_script]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_coordinator_script}::
Path to the coordinator script (workbench/testbench) that handles the dispatched command.

[[burd_launcher]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_launcher}::
Path to the launcher bootstrap script, set by the tabtarget before exec.

[[burd_station_file]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_station_file}::
Absolute path to the developer's station regime assignment file.
Set by the launcher from BURC_STATION_FILE.

[[burd_term_cols]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burd_term_cols}::
Terminal column width at dispatch time.
Detected from stty if available, defaults to 80.

==== Computed Variables

[[burd_group_computed]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_computed}::
Variables derived during dispatch setup.

[[burd_now_stamp]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_now_stamp}::
Unique timestamp combining date, time, PID, and random component.

[[burd_now_epoch]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burd_now_epoch}::
UTC epoch seconds captured from same `date` invocation as {burd_now_stamp}.

[[burd_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_temp_dir}::
Ephemeral directory for this dispatch invocation.

[[burd_output_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_output_dir}::
Fixed output directory cleared and recreated on each dispatch.

[[burd_transcript]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_transcript}::
Path to the transcript file within the temporary directory.

[[burd_git_context]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_git_context}::
Git describe output capturing repository state at dispatch time.

[[burd_log_last]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_last}::
Path to the "last" log file, overwritten each dispatch.

[[burd_log_same]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_same}::
Path to the curated "same" log file for the command+target combination.

[[burd_log_hist]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_hist}::
Path to the timestamped historical log file.

==== Parsed Variables

[[burd_group_parsed]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_parsed}::
Variables extracted from the tabtarget filename during argument processing.

[[burd_command]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_command}::
Primary command token (first token from tabtarget split).

[[burd_target]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_target}::
Complete tabtarget string as received by dispatch.

[[burd_cli_args]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_repeated
{burd_cli_args}::
Array of additional CLI arguments passed after the tabtarget.

[[burd_token_1]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_1}::
Positional token 1 from tabtarget split (the colophon).

[[burd_token_2]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_2}::
Positional token 2 from tabtarget split (the frontispiece).

[[burd_token_3]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_3}::
Positional token 3 from tabtarget split (the imprint, if present).

[[burd_token_4]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_4}::
Positional token 4 from tabtarget split.

[[burd_token_5]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_5}::
Positional token 5 from tabtarget split.

=== BURE Regime

[[bure_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{bure_regime}::
Ambient regime for caller-supplied behavioral overrides to BUK primitives.
Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURE variables are set directly in the caller's environment.
Kindled and validated when buc_command.sh is sourced.

[[bure_countdown]]
//axvr_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_countdown}::
Countdown behavior override.
When set to `skip`, buc_countdown bypasses interactive countdown and tty output.
When unset, countdown proceeds normally with interactive tty cancel window.
Any other value fails validation.

[[bure_verbose]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{bure_verbose}::
Verbosity level controlling diagnostic output.
0=silent, 1=show messages, 2=enable bash trace.

[[bure_color]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{bure_color}::
Color output policy.
Input as auto/0/1; resolved to 0 or 1 during dispatch.
Respects NO_COLOR environment convention.

==== Tweak Mechanism

The tweak mechanism provides a single caller-supplied name/value override that flows through invocation without BUK interpreting its semantics.
BUK enrolls, validates, and propagates the pair; consuming kits (e.g., RBK) check the name and act on the value.

Constraint: one tweak per invocation.
The caller sets at most one
{bure_tweak_name}/{bure_tweak_value}
pair in the environment before invoking a tabtarget.
The pair is ambient — it propagates through `zbuto_invoke` like all BURE variables, without requiring re-kindle in the invoked command.

[[bure_tweak_name]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_tweak_name}::
Consumer-interpreted tweak identifier.
The name selects which behavioral override the consuming kit applies.
BUK validates length (0–64 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
+
Example: `graft_image` — tells RBK to substitute the graft source image.

[[bure_tweak_value]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_tweak_value}::
Consumer-interpreted tweak payload.
The value is opaque to BUK; its format and interpretation are defined by whatever kit checks
{bure_tweak_name}.
BUK validates length (0–256 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
+
Either variable may be set independently — BUK does not enforce pairing.
Consumer kits that require both must enforce that themselves.

==== Exchange Labels

[[bure_label]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_xname axd_optional
{bure_label}::
Correlation label for cross-project exchange.
When set, propagated verbatim into
{burx_label}
during dispatch.
When unset, BUD writes empty string.
Maximum 120 characters, xname format.
+
BUD writes whatever
{bure_label}
contains without interpreting the semantics.

=== BURX Regime

[[burx_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burx_prefix}::
Bash Utility Exchange Regime

[[burx_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{burx_regime}::
Exchange regime for cross-project dispatch state.
Written by bud_dispatch.sh to both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}
as `burx.env` via
{buf_write_fact_single}.
Unlike single-project regimes,
{burx_regime}
is designed to be read across a remote project boundary.
+
Variables appear in two phases:
initial fields are written at dispatch start;
terminal fields are appended at dispatch completion.
Absence of
{burx_exit_status}
indicates the dispatch is still running.

[[burx_pid]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burx_pid}::
Process ID of the dispatched command.

[[burx_began_at]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burx_began_at}::
Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch started.
Format: `YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.NNNNNNNNN`.

[[burx_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burx_tabtarget}::
The tabtarget that was dispatched.
Corresponds to
{burd_target}.

[[burx_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_temp_dir}::
Absolute path to the dispatch temporary directory.
Corresponds to
{burd_temp_dir}.

[[burx_transcript]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_transcript}::
Absolute path to the transcript file.
Corresponds to
{burd_transcript}.

[[burx_log_hist]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_log_hist}::
Absolute path to the historical log file.
Corresponds to
{burd_log_hist}.

[[burx_label]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burx_label}::
Correlation label from
{bure_label}.
Always present — empty string when
{bure_label}
is not set.
Format and interpretation are opaque to BUD.

[[burx_exit_status]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer axd_optional
{burx_exit_status}::
Unix exit code of the dispatched command.
Absence indicates the dispatch is still running.

[[burx_ended_at]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burx_ended_at}::
Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch completed.
Same format as
{burx_began_at}.
Present only when
{burx_exit_status}
is present.

include::BUSD-DispatchRuntime.adoc[]

== Remote Node Access

Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.

=== Privilege Tiers

The {burn_tier} field of every {burn_regime} profile classifies the profile
as either `privileged` or `workload`. Tabtargets refuse aliases of the wrong
tier at entry.

==== Privileged Tier

Privileged profiles authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or equivalent
privileges. Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user
provisioning, node-establishment ceremonies.

Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.

==== Workload Tier

Workload profiles authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
privileged authority. Used for normal remote work: running commands,
file operations, interactive shells.

Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
station users. Lifecycle is short:
{busn_conscript}
when needed,
{busn_discharge}
when done.

=== Tabtarget Catalog

Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.

==== Lifecycle

`buw-rnl`::
List all {burn_regime} profiles in the local station subtree.

`buw-rnr <alias>`::
Render a single {burn_regime} profile.

`buw-rnv <alias>`::
Validate a single {burn_regime} profile.

==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)

`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x} <host> <user> <moniker>`::
{busn_garrison} a node for the named platform.
Writes the privileged {burn_regime} profile and runs the platform-specific
ceremony to establish admin trust.
Platform letters: l=Linux, m=Mac, c=Cygwin, w=WSL, p=PowerShell, x=Localhost.

==== Workload (privileged authority required)

`buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x} <privileged-alias> <workload-moniker>`::
{busn_conscript} a workload user via the named privileged BURN.
Creates the remote OS user, installs the station user's pubkey, and
writes a workload {burn_regime} profile locally.

`buw-nwd <workload-alias>`::
{busn_discharge} a workload user.
Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
authority and removes the local profile.

`buw-nwi <privileged-alias>`::
{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
privileged BURN.

==== Workload (operational)

`buw-nwc <workload-alias>`::
Check responsiveness via passwordless SSH probe.

`buw-nwr <workload-alias> <cmd-file>`::
Run the contents of `<cmd-file>` on the remote node; capture output to
{burd_output_dir}.

`buw-nws <workload-alias>`::
Open an interactive SSH session.

==== Handbook

`buw-hn0`::
Top-level handbook landing for node access procedures.

=== Privileges

The {burs_user} owns its profile subtree at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/`
exclusively — operations write only under the current station user's tree.

A {burn_regime} profile describes a connection identity (host, user, shell,
alias, key, tier) — not project state, not work-directory. Project context
is supplied by the consumer at invocation time.

Each platform subdoc declares the remote-user privilege contract that its
ceremony assumes (e.g., on Windows the privileged user must be a member of
the Administrators group).

=== Verb Definitions

[[busn_garrison]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_garrison}::
Establish privileged BURN trust on a node by writing the privileged
{burn_regime} profile and running the platform-specific ceremony to gain
admin access. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-npg{platform}`. First-time
node setup; idempotent on re-run.

[[busn_conscript]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_conscript}::
Create an ephemeral workload user on a node and provision a workload
{burn_regime} profile locally. Requires a privileged BURN as authority for
the remote-user creation. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-nwc{platform}`,
where the platform letter selects the workload shell — independent of the
privileged BURN's shell choice.

[[busn_discharge]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_discharge}::
Remove a workload user from a node and delete the local workload
{burn_regime} profile. The workload profile encodes its privileged
authority; {busn_discharge} uses that authority to delete the remote OS
user. Tabtarget: `buw-nwd`.

[[busn_inventory]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_inventory}::
List workload users currently provisioned on a node, queried via a
privileged BURN. Scope is the node's full workload-user population — not
just the current station user's history. Tabtarget: `buw-nwi`.
